data.title: loanDepot: A 'Buy' With External And Internal Tailwinds (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become bullish on loanDepot, Inc. (LDI).

LDI will benefit from the "Government-Sponsored Enterprises/GSEs" purchase of "Mortgage-Backed Securities/MBS" and potentially new investment restrictions for "Single-Family Rentals/SFRs."

Internally, I'm impressed with its in-house servicing loan strategy and the willingness to embrace AI.

My prior March 17, 2024, write-up touched on its worse-than-anticipated 4Q23 showing and a possible 2H24 upturn.

Fannie And Fred Are Enlarging Their Balance Sheets

A Jan. 9, 2026, SA News piece highlighted that Trump is "instructing unnamed representatives to acquire $200B in mortgage bonds." The article also mentioned the president's earlier remarks on how "he'll take steps to ban large institutional investors from buying single-family homes." I will discuss the first development here, and address the other event in the next section.

I think the purchase of MBS by GSEs will be good for LDI.

The $0.2 trillion is a meaningful amount. That's roughly the same as Baird's projected "net supply of new mortgages" for this year cited in a Dec 18, 2025 AAM commentary.

Last week, "the 30-Year Fixed Mortgage Rate" fell "below 6%" for "the first time since February 2023" according to SA analyst "Hoya Capital's" Jan. 11 update.

My opinion is that LDI's prospects have become more promising, thanks to lower rates and tighter mortgage spreads.

The firm drew attention to its transformation from "a de novo start-up in 2010 to at one point becoming the second-largest retail lender in the country" at its 3Q25 briefing.

UBS Group's (UBS) January 9 research note (not publicly available) titled "Implications Of The $200B MBS Purchase Directive" quantified the effects of the rate environment changes.

The incremental refinance volume brought by Fannie's and Fred's moves is estimated to be $0.45 trillion, based on UBS's calculations. That assumes a 6ppts (percentage points) rise in the proportion of "the market in-the-money."

LDI's 10-Q filing indicated that its "loan refinancing volumes" are "primarily driven by fluctuations in mortgage loan interest rates."

SFRs Could Become Off-Limits For Wall Street

Individuals may no longer have to compete with REITs and PE firms in the single-family space if the president gets his way as outlined in the preceding section.

Morgan Stanley (MS) issued a report (inaccessible to the public) named "Thoughts On SFRs" on Jan 8 assessing this possibility. MS is forecasting "$40 billion of new mortgage originations", implying a "3% increase in annual US purchase originations."

The institutions don't take up mortgages from LDI or its peers to finance their single-family purchases. They have alternative funding sources such as bonds or revolvers.

You can imagine that keeping PEs/REITs out of this segment will translate into greater mortgage-related demand from occupiers. MS's expectations of enhanced origination growth are realistic in my view.

Things can get even better for LDI in certain scenarios.

The sell-side has speculated on the likelihood of some REITs being "forced to liquidate or decided to liquidate (their single-family properties) due to an inability to scale their portfolios."

In the case above, the housing inventory additions could depress prices and attract individual homebuyers back to the market. LDI will most probably witness more loan inquiries if that happens.

Spotlight On Self-Help Measures

LDI isn't solely reliant on regulations or policies to improve its future performance.

At its 3rd quarter call, it stressed the importance of exploiting "positive operating leverage." Its goal is to enjoy "both the benefit of increased volume and increased margin" when "volumes return."

LDI's corporate slides draw attention to its "strong recapture rate at 65%," and a "$0 customer acquisition cost" for new business from its existing borrower base.

The firm revealed at the Q3 meeting that it "made an investment to bring servicing in-house" in '20. That has boosted its top line and bottom line through low attrition and optimized marketing expenses, respectively.

It has taken other actions to support its sales. These include sponsoring sports events (e.g. MLB/NHL) and working closely with property developers for referrals.

Separately, "Artificial Intelligence/AI" has turned into a critical profitability lever.

One example is the automation of LDI's call centers, so that fewer human operators are needed.

Management's 3Q25 briefing comments suggest that it's also "pivoting the use of new and emerging technologies across sales, operations, and software engineering" to "lower our cost of production."

The market sees LDI reversing from a net loss of $0.06 billion in FY25 to register +$56M earnings for FY26 (source: S&P Capital IQ). This is consistent with the multiple tailwinds described in this write-up.

You Should Be Mindful Of These Risks

The SFR-related ban might not materialize if Trump's proposal is challenged in court.

LDI may not execute well on internal initiatives, causing the actual revenue boost or margin expansion to be less substantial than hoped for.

My Concluding Remarks

LDI's rating is revised from "Hold" to "Buy."

I assign a target P/B of 3.4 times to this name. That implies a +42% upside (3.4/2.4), considering its current valuation ratio of 2.4x.

I've used the "Gordon Growth Model." My inputs are a 21% ROE (FY27 consensus), a +3% growth (mature economy proxy), and an 8.3% cost of equity (financial services sector average).

As per my analysis, there are favorable developments within and outside the company, which can act as re-rating triggers.

data.title: FTA Aviation Series C Preferred Shares Remain A Hold Despite Juicy 8.1%
data.description:
I covered FTAI Aviation’s Preferred Series C shares (NASDAQ:FTAIN) with a hold rating in my last report. The stock price has remained flat since then with a 1.8% total return compared to the S&P 500’s 7.9%. So, you would have been better off putting the money into an S&P 500 tracker, but at the same time the preferred shares are more of a fixed income idea for which I believe comparisons to the S&P 500 performance are not necessarily meaningful. It should be noted that interest rate environments and the possibility of redemption of the shares plays a substantial role. In this report, I discuss whether FTAI Aviation Preferred Series C shares are currently more attractive with a focus on the interest rate environment and how possible redemption of shares limits upside which leads me to maintain my hold rating.

About The FTAI Series C Preferred Series C Yields 8.1%

FTAI Series C Preferred Stock was issued in March 2021 with a par value of $25, generating an annual dividend of $2.0625 paid quarterly. Currently, the stock is trading at $25.43, bringing the effective yield to 8.1%. This is unchanged from my prior report.

The preferred shares are redeemable on or after June 2026. The shares have a cumulative dividend, which means that if FTAI Aviation misses a dividend payment, the dividend still has to be paid eventually. However, so far, FTAI Aviation has not missed a single dividend payment to preferred shareholders. The shares can be redeemed before 2026 in the event FTAI Aviation is downgraded by all rating agencies.

Redeeming The Preferred Shares Remains The Base Case Scenario

The yield of more than 8% does look attractive, but given that the stock is redeemable there are some concerns on share price appreciation. The Series C yield is structured as a fixed-rate reset nature. In June 2026, the yield will be remeasured against the five-year Treasury rate plus a 7.378 point spread.

Currently the 5-year Treasury rate is 3.75%. If current interest rates do not drastically change, the yield would be 11.1%. Previously, the five-year treasury yield was below the fed fund rate. That is no longer the case. The fed fund rate is currently 3.64% which is 11 basis points below the 5-year Treasury rate. With a relatively small spread between the two, it means that cuts are expected but not in an aggressive way.

The current expectation is that by June 2026, when the Preferred Series C has its rate reset, the fed fund rate will be around 325-350 basis points. This is unchanged from my prior assessment. If we take the mid-point of the target range, we get to 3.375%. Together with the 11 basis points spread, it would imply a five-year treasury rate of around 3.5%. Adding the 7.378 points spread, we would get to a new yield of 10.86% indicating a 10.7% effective yield using the current price of the preferred shares of $25.43.

Currently the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread indicates that investors are looking for a 2.76 points spread on top of the five-year treasury rate implying a 6.5% yield that investors are looking for. In the case of redemption, investors will likely get two more dividend payments of $0.5156 and at current prices there will be a $0.43 loss from redemption bringing the effective annualized yield to 4.8%. So, the Series C shares do not look particularly attractive.

Even if the company would consider going through the rate reset, we note that the lower interest rate environment is tightening spreads that investors are looking for and as a result rather than a prospective effective 10.7% yield it would make a lot of sense to redeem the stock as there is a substantial spread between the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread and the effective yield FTAI’s preferred stock would offer.

If the Fed modestly eases rates, there could be a 75-125 basis point spread between the fed fund rate. Taking the mid-point would give a 4.33% Treasury rate. This would send the yield to 11.7% next year. However, I believe that the 5-year Treasury rate will continue to signal expectations of rate cuts, and as a result, I would not be surprised if the yield would reset at 10%. Furthermore, we note that the company already called the Series B in 2025 and the Series A in 2024. So, redeeming the Series C lies in line with expectations.

Typical Spreads On 5-Year Treasury Support Redemption

FTAI Aviation stock has a BB+ rating since November 2025 while the preferred shares have a credit rating of BB-. For BB- preferred shares, a typical spread compared to the 5-year treasury rate would be 200-400 basis points. This indicates a 5.75%-7.75% yield. This would imply a price target between $27.50-$35.86. So, the current yield is substantially above what would be typical for BB- rated preferred stock and the prospective reset yield would also be substantially above that. All of this supports that redemption of the preferred shares is likely and simultaneously removes the upside to $27.50-$35.86 derived from a 5.75%-7.75% yield range. As a result, I deem the stock a hold.

Conclusion: FTAI Aviation Series C Preferred Stock Continues To Be A Hold

FTAI Aviation continues to offer a juicy 8% yield, but that is not a reason to buy the stock in my view at current prices as a possible redemption would put the yield at just 4.8%. The best course of action would actually be to continue holding the stock, collect the two remaining dividend payments and after that sell the stock if the price is still above $25 anticipating a redemption. If the price is below $25, continue holding the stock until redemption.

data.title: Gilead Sciences: From All-Time Highs To Higher Highs
data.description:
Since my article from October, "What To Expect From Gilead in Q3 2025", Gilead Sciences' stock (GILD) reached an all-time high of $128.70.

However, after November 20, a corrective pattern called a zigzag started to form. Currently, I expect it to end in mid-January. After which, according to my assessment, Wave 5 will resume upon reaching the +2σ band formed from the VWAP, namely $134.50.

And since I want to be as objective as possible, I ask myself: Why do I see Gilead as having an attractive risk/reward profile?

The launch of Yeztugo strengthened Gilead's HIV franchise

First of all, I want to note that Gilead actively captures the HIV treatment market, even without taking into account Yeztugo. I see this through sales of its three key drugs, Biktarvy, Descovy, and Yeztugo.

So, the big-selling HIV medication called Biktarvy generated $3.69 billion in revenue for Gilead in Q3, up 6.2% year-over-year, and beat my "base case" scenario by $65 million.

Returning to Yeztugo [lenacapavir], it was approved by the FDA and the EC on June 18 and August 26, respectively, as pre-exposure prophylaxis against HIV.

Sales of Yeztugo were $39 million in Q3, 2.6 times higher than the previous quarter.

And on December 3, CFO Andrew Dickinson expressed great confidence in lenacapavir at a healthcare conference organized by Citigroup (C). He also disclosed, to my delight, that Yeztugo sales are expected to be about $100 million in Q4.

A couple of things that I'd highlight our J-code for reimbursement in the U.S. came -- it was effective October 1. It came a couple of months earlier than expected. We hit 75% payer covered lives, and that's both across commercial as well as government payers.

A couple of months earlier than expected as well, which is great. And we guided to roughly $150 million of sales for the first half of this year, including a little less than $100 million of expected sales for the fourth quarter. So it's off to a great start.

This is just the beginning of what we expect to be kind of a steady, consistent, durable growth in this business in Yeztugo, in particular, over time.

So, I can call its launch extremely successful. And the secret behind this success lies in lenacapavir's competitive advantages over GSK's Cabenuva/Dovato (GSK) and Johnson & Johnson's Edurant (JNJ).

I already talked about them in the article "Gilead Sciences: HIV Leadership Outshines Decline in COVID-19 Drug Sales."

But to provide a more comprehensive picture of the HIV market, I've also added other drugs to the table below.

And a key milestone last year related to the lenacapavir development program was the publication on December 15 of the results from the Phase 3 ARTISTRY-2 study [NCT06333808].

Gilead reported that the combination of [BIC] bictegravir 75 mg, an integrase inhibitor and a component of Biktarvy, with [LEN] lenacapavir 50 mg was statistically non-inferior to Biktarvy.

And given that BIC+LEN met the primary endpoint, non-inferior to multi-tablet regimens, in the ARTISTRY-1 trial [NCT05502341], I believe this combination will be approved by the FDA in the first quarter of 2027.

GILD is not stopping there and is evaluating lenacapavir as HIV prevention with once-yearly injections or as a combo with Merck's Islatravir (MRK), monoclonal antibodies [teropavimab and zinlirvimab].

Sales of Gilead's Livdelzi grew more than I factored into my DCF model

Besides its HIV franchise, it has a diversified portfolio of drugs targeting liver diseases. One of these is Livdelzi [seladelpar], a PPAR-delta agonist.

Since the acquisition of seladelpar, as a part of the deal with CymaBay Therapeutics, in my view, it has become the preferred option among hepatologists for the treatment of PBC [primary biliary cholangitis].

I don't want to repeat all the reasons that led to the changes in the clinical practice to treat this autoimmune liver disease. But in short, Livdelzi is more effective in reducing pruritus, ALT, and alkaline phosphatase levels compared to Iqirvo's Ipsen/Genfit (IPSEY) (GNFTY) and Ocaliva. By the way, Alfasigma withdrew Ocaliva from the US and EU markets.

As a result, in Q3, sales of Livdelzi rose 28.2% quarter-on-quarter to $100 million versus Iqirvo's €48.6 million. Although I remind you that Iqirvo was launched three months earlier, meaning it had a first-mover advantage.

Also, CCO Johanna Mercier sounded pretty optimistic on the Q3 call when she talked about Livdelzi.

Livdelzi grew 35% sequentially, driven by strong commercial execution, including some new launches outside the U.S. and withdrawal of a competitor's product in the U.S. We are particularly pleased to see strong levels of persistence among users and believe Livdelzi shows clear differentiation and value to those with PBC.

Risks

And again, risks.

I have mixed feelings about Gilead's attempts to compete with Merck, AstraZeneca (AZN), and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) in the oncology market. It doesn't have the same experience in developing anti-cancer drugs as this 'trio'.

And even its acquisition of Kite Pharma for $11.9 billion back on August 28, 2017, hasn't brought Gilead any closer to its goals in this therapeutic area.

So, total sales of its two CAR T-cell therapies, Yescarta and Tecartus, fell by 10.9% YoY to $432 million in Q3.

In my opinion, the weakening demand for them reflects tough competition from more effective drugs for large B-cell lymphoma, such as Roche's Columvi (RHHBY) and Breyanzi.

Takeaway

Gilead started 2026 on a high note.

And according to Seeking Alpha, it will announce its Q4 figures next month, on February 10.

To my regret, Gilead itself has not yet specified the date, but has only informed investors that acquired IPR&D expenses will be $539 million in this quarter.

The consensus analyst estimate for GILD's EPS is $1.89, and revenue is $7.68 billion. However, I think Wall Street is again setting the bar low, as it has in eight of the last 11 quarters.

Overall, as a reminder.

Gilead's non-GAAP EPS reached $2.47 in Q3. It rose by 22.3% YoY and 22.9% QoQ, thanks to medications which I mentioned earlier, and also Vemlidy and Trodelvy.

And given the strong results from the iMMagine-1 Phase 2 trial published on December 6, in which anito-cel, developed jointly with Arcellx (ACLX), has demonstrated efficacy similar to J&J/Legend Biotech's Carvykti (LEGN) in the treatment of RRMM, I continue to cover Gilead with a 'Buy' rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Duolingo Has Plunged, But There's No Opportunity Here Yet
data.description:
I was looking through a list of the biggest losing stocks in recent months, and Duolingo, Inc. (DUOL) stood out to me.

I love consumer tech companies that can become a platform for a given space. I missed Netflix (NFLX), despite enjoying the service because I thought it was overvalued during its aggressive growth stage. However, I did not make the same mistake twice; I bought Spotify (SPOT) early in its days as a public company and enjoyed sizable upside there. I have also been quite bullish on Unity Software (U), which, I believe, can be a picks-and-shovels play for the video games space and capture a significant chunk of that TAM over the next decade.

With that context in mind, Duolingo is a similarly interesting company. In theory, it can grab a sizable piece of the language-learning market, and even adjacent learning spaces such as music, computer programming, and chess. Duolingo is reporting rapid revenue growth and has a well-known brand. And now, with DUOL stock down more than 50% over the past six months, shares are on sale compared to prior prices. So, what's not to like?

Valuation Remains Demanding

It's easy to look at a stock chart like Duolingo's with a steep plunge and think that it must be approaching buy-the-dip territory. And, after all, the stock is now selling at a reported just 20x forward earnings. That's a decent entry point, right?

But that P/E ratio is misleading. The company enjoyed a huge tax benefit last quarter, leading to Duolingo reporting $5.95 of earnings in Q3. This beat expectations by a whopping $5.19 per share. You don't get that level of earnings beat simply from stronger-than-expected operating results.

And indeed, the confounding factor here was that the company reported $246 million in gains related to income tax. By contrast, Duolingo only earned $46 million of income from operations during the quarter. Excluding the tax effect, Q3 EPS would have been approximately 93 cents per share. And that figure would be further reduced if you backed out share-based comp from the mix.

Putting stock-based compensation aside, on an adjusted earnings basis, here is what analysts are forecasting going forward:

Once the one-time tax effect rolls off, we're looking at about 40x adjusted forward earnings for 2026 and 33x earnings for 2027.

On an EBITDA basis, Duolingo remains exceedingly expensive:

Just because the stock used to trade at 250x EBITDA doesn't necessarily guarantee that it is a bargain at 57x EBITDA today.

Duolingo: A Gaming Company?

In a recent note, Bank of America suggested that there could be significant upside for Duolingo. The report argues that Duolingo is an entertainment company and that it has two addressable markets, one for learning and one for casual gaming. It goes so far as to suggest that Duolingo could become one of the world's top five mobile gaming apps, putting it in competition with Roblox (RBLX) and Candy Crush.

There is a grain of truth here. Duolingo is a lot more entertaining than earlier versions of language learning software. I was short language software company Rosetta Stone, back when it was a publicly traded entity, in significant part because I felt it was both ineffective in teaching languages and also not particularly engaging for users.

While Duolingo (in my opinion anyway) is not especially compelling in terms of how well it can actually teach a language, it is certainly one of the more entertaining apps in the learning space.

That said, I have kids. I sometimes let them borrow my tablet to play games for practicing math or writing skills. But, unless I keep a close eye on them, they'll end up playing some mobile game with no redeeming educational value rather than the learning one.

I simply don't believe that, for most users, Duolingo is sufficiently entertaining on its own to be true competition to lazy scrolling time that would otherwise be spent on Netflix, TikTok, or Candy Crush. For someone inherently motivated to learn languages, chess, or music, Duolingo is a suitable option. It's going to be more entertaining than reading a book or going to a college class that teaches that subject, sure. But is it a direct competitor to Roblox for pure pleasure-seeking mobile gaming experiences? I don't buy it.

I will grant that Duolingo is quite addictive; I know several people who have felt an almost compulsive need to keep their Duolingo streak going. It's certainly something to have users want to come back every day to keep a streak going. Just look at companies like Snapchat (SNAP) that have been able to harness that tendency for proof of this.

Yet, I fear that it is cheap engagement.

Wells Fargo recently downgraded Duolingo stock, arguing that the company's brand is weakening, and that users are rejecting Duolingo's more aggressive monetization efforts. One independent analyst also worked through Duolingo's year-in-review data and concluded that most users are spending just a couple of minutes a day or less on the app.

I would love to see Duolingo provide more granular information around its user behavior. Because, unless proven otherwise, my hunch is that many, if not most, users are active just long enough to keep their streaks going and aren't actually using the app for long periods of time regularly. This, in turn, implies that Duolingo could struggle to get a large portion of its free-tier users to eventually upgrade.

As an analogy here, I have a long daily activity streak in a puzzle game called Wordle. But I spend about one minute a day on that app and would never think of buying a subscription for it. A user can be active without being valuable.

And indeed, as of Q3 data, fewer than 10% of monthly active users pay for a Duolingo subscription, which seems like a fairly lackluster result for an app that bulls say has such high user engagement. Meanwhile, advertising makes up less than 10% of Duolingo's total revenues. The vast majority of the user base is contributing hardly anything to the company's bottom-line results, as they don't pay for a subscription, and ad revenues are not moving the needle for Duolingo.

Simply put, I don't think people are treating Duolingo like they would a highly pleasurable mobile game such as Candy Crush. If the company can't get a lot more paid subs or greatly increase ad revenues from free users, this growth story is not going to meet bullish expectations.

AI: Friend Or Foe?

Some bears believe Duolingo will quickly become irrelevant because of AI. In a world with perfect machine translation, what's the point of learning another language?

I can say that I started learning Spanish, as an adult, in 2010. Already, at that time, people told me I was wasting my time because we'd have perfect translators on our phones within five or ten years. It's now 2026, and while real-time phone translation has advanced greatly, it hasn't come anywhere close to making language learning irrelevant.

Phone translation is useful for tourists doing stuff like deciphering restaurant menus or street signs. But are people using real-time phone translation for more demanding activities such as doctor's appointments, legal functions, buying real estate, etc. in other countries? I've lived in Latin America for more than 10 years now, and I've hardly ever seen foreigners try to use phone translation for serious conversations or business. Folks still either learn the language or hire a person to interpret for them.

And even for everyday conversation, most people simply don't seem that inclined to try to talk to each other via phone translation to break through the language barrier. Maybe other countries and regions are different, but here in Latin America, I just don't see it in daily life. There's so much nuance to slang and local idioms, not to mention the body language and other non-verbal functions of communication that are lost with phone-based communication rather than verbally talking to each other.

Long story short, translation will keep getting better, but most people moving to another country or learning a language for work purposes aren't going to outsource that to a phone in the immediate future.

A bearish counterpoint, however, would be that many people learn languages (especially English) for work reasons, and a whole range of jobs associated with this could become obsolete. Think of people in emerging markets learning English to work in call centers, perform IT jobs, or serve tourists at hotels, restaurants, etc. It's not difficult to imagine much of that, particularly call centers, getting displaced by AI within a few years. In that case, many people will never bother to learn English in the first place if doing so no longer leads to decent employment prospects.

There's also the possibility that AI will allow other companies to code up better alternatives to Duolingo. The moat around software in general seems to be shrinking, and there's a ton of capital going after gamified versions of all sorts of things. Duolingo is a first mover here, but as development cycles get faster, Duolingo's edge could shrink.

Duolingo is leaning heavily into AI to improve its own courses and workflows. The company introduced dozens of new languages, for example, with lessons that were built in part with AI tools rather than human translators. That could be a good way to offer instruction for less popular languages that would otherwise fall through the cracks.

That said, there was dramatic consumer backlash as Duolingo applied more AI methods to its overall learning flow.

In April, Duolingo announced its new AI-first strategy, while also disclosing that it would reduce the number of humans that it employed. Here's Duolingo's stock price since the AI-first push was revealed to the public.

The company's engagement metrics almost immediately decelerated once the AI-first memo was published, and the stock price soon followed suit.

This came to light with the company's underwhelming Q2 earnings results. CEO Luis Alfonso von Ahn Arellano acknowledged that they underperformed expectations directly due to the AI backlash:

We came in at 40% (DAU growth), which is slightly on the lower end of what we thought. Everything is -- we feel very strong about this. The reason we came towards the lower end was because I said some stuff about AI, and this was -- I didn't give enough context. And because of that, we got, particularly in our social media, we got some backlash on it.

There are numerous software companies, Adobe (ADBE) being another great example, where you can make a perfectly valid case that AI will both harm and hurt their business, and it's a question of which effect will ultimately be greater. For Duolingo specifically, AI should make it easier to produce additional learning content. Additionally, in a more competitive job market, people need more skills to secure employment. This could drive more engagement with smart learning tools.

Duolingo's CEO has also used far-reaching descriptions of how AI tutors and app-based learning could overtake human teaching in the future. I'm quite skeptical this will happen anytime in the near term. But, if it, in fact, does become possible and Duolingo is the company to achieve this, it would likely mean tremendous returns for the company and its shareholders.

I don't believe AI is going to immediately make language learning obsolete, though it will reduce the number of casual learners who only want to pick up a few phrases before a vacation. It could meaningfully shrink the TAM longer-term, though, especially for folks learning English as a second language for career purposes. Duolingo could also suffer (additional) reputational damage from rolling out poorly designed AI tools or AI-related messaging in the future.

My overall view is that AI will be neutral to slightly unfavorable to Duolingo over the next five years. But the range of potential outcomes here is huge, and any opinion I have here can change at a moment's notice, given how quickly the technology is advancing.

Duolingo Stock Verdict

I get the bullish case here. There's the potential for this to become the Netflix or Spotify of the app-based learning space. Language learning is a large market, and even more so if Duolingo can carve out a decent profile in teaching other fields such as computer programming, music, etc.

Despite the company's recent AI messaging missteps, revenue growth remains strong. And the firm is meaningfully profitable and cash flow generative.

You can sketch out a realistic case where Duolingo continues to post 25%+ annualized revenue for at least a few more years and exceeds the market's current expectations. I don't think this is a high probability, mind you, but it's certainly within the range of plausible outcomes if Duolingo plays its AI cards right and can move into adjacent learning markets as well. And if the gaming argument is correct and Duolingo is actually the next Roblox, the sky is the limit for the business' growth profile.

The issue I have in underwriting this, however, is I see that full bullish case as only something like a 10%-20% probability outcome. Throw in another 20% chance that the stock does okay-to-reasonably well, and I'd guess that in the other 60%-70% of potential future scenarios, Duolingo badly trails the market. I'm not really keen on these sorts of moonshot investments, where I have a small chance of hitting a jackpot and a large chance of underperforming the market.

At $500/share earlier this year, I don't see how DUOL stock could have possibly lived up to expectations. Shares were priced beyond perfection at more than 250x EBITDA.

I can, however, imagine an outcome where Duolingo grows into a real $500 per share valuation over the next five years, in which case you could get roughly a triple from buying at today's less exuberant price. That'd be excellent.

But, if I had to bet, I think it's more likely that this stock is trading under $100/share five years from now than it is to be over $500. Be it AI disruption, newer and better apps coming along, a persistent decline in user engagement, or whatnot, there are various ways for Duolingo's growth thesis to fail.

And at a starting price of around 40x forward earnings, 50x EBITDA, and 7x revenues, there's little margin for safety here. As such, even after the sharp sell-off in recent months, the odds favor more downside ahead.

data.title: On Holding: Running Like Swiss Clockwork
data.description:
Recap

The Swiss premium sports brand On Holding AG (ONON) is up 29% since I first covered it here on Seeking Alpha back in May 2024, roughly in line with the S&P 500’s gain of 31%. My conclusion was clearly leaning positively on the back of excellent profitable growth but a high valuation, which could nevertheless have been justifiable given the growth dynamics. I came to this conclusion by crafting an integrated financial model and partly backwards-engineering market participants’ long-term growth assumptions.

After well over a year and a half, I am providing a simplified update regarding On’s still-outstanding positioning and execution.

Unlocking Multiple Growth Opportunities

On Holding has long communicated its two key growth opportunities: regional expansion and category expansion. Both are working out as hoped. The company percentage of sales in the Americas came down to 58% from 64% for the first nine months of the year. This was attributed to explosive growth of more than 100% in Asia-Pacific and dynamic growth in the 30s in EMEA. When we talk about growth around the 20s as the weakest region (Americas), we are certainly dealing with an excellent growth profile through and through.

Nevertheless, the continuously strong Swiss Franc weighs on all reported revenues, with the US-Dollar weakness causing the largest discrepancy between constant-currency growth and reported growth. Yet, On’s growth as of today is impressive enough to essentially offset currency headwinds.

Apparel and Accessories sales have been roughly doubling during the nine-month period, while Shoes grew around the 30% range. Here again, it looks impressive how despite continued dynamic growth in Shoes, their revenue share continues to drop in the direction of 90%, as Apparel and Accessories catch up so strongly, albeit starting from relatively low levels.

Regarding sales channels, too, there is no weakness in sight, with DTC slightly outgrowing wholesale but not significantly. The latest channel mix was approximately 60:40 wholesale:DTC. DTC growth reflects extraordinary brand strength, as Nike, on the other hand, had to soften its aggressive DTC push and re-establish wholesale partnerships in order to remain relevant and visible.

With that, On remains “one of the young new market participants with the potential to threaten even Nike in the long term,” as I wrote last time. As laid out in my recent Nike article based on Piper Sandler data, Nike has been losing significant US-teens’ mind share recently. In fact, On’s TTM revenue of $3.6 billion already represents 8% that of Nike’s $46.5 billion. This is an impressive run-up in relative market share to the market leader, as visible below. On the other hand, I am not at all writing off Nike, and any successful brand revitalization could dampen On’s growth story.

Improving Profitability

Please refer to my key financials overview below for the following:

A – After annual growth rates of at least 60% until 2023, top-line growth has briefly slowed to 20% in 2024 but has notably reaccelerated above 40% lately.

B – At the same time, On impressively boasts expanding gross margins solidly above the 60% mark with 62% on a TTM basis and an upwardly-revised target of 62.5% for the fiscal year. This is far above Nike’s gross margins in the 40s and underscores On’s strong premium standing. As such, On’s gross margin reality is also notably better than I had modeled in 2024, when I assumed it would gradually approach Nike’s.

C – On’s operating margin, with 12% on a TTM basis, is already in the realm of Nike’s best years and I continue to expect it to expand significantly through economies of scale as the company’s growth progresses. On the other hand, On might also reach a point where competitive headwinds might increase and must be offset by increased marketing spend.

D – Next, my assumption was that On’s strong internal financing power thanks to its profitability will notably decrease the need for shareholder dilution. Indeed, diluted share count has increased by only 1% annually over the last three years.

F – Free cash flow conversion from operating income has stabilized around or above the 100% mark during all of the past three years, indicating healthy maturing and scaling of the company. TTM FCF of $440 million represents a 2.8% yield against On’s current enterprise value of $15.7 billion, equivalent to a 36x EV/FCF multiple. Given the company’s growth, this multiple does not appear too demanding this time.

Continued: More On Valuation

I am not conducting a comprehensive DCF valuation this time, but I acknowledge the following changes are positively and negatively affecting discount parameters. First, raw company beta came down from a 24-month and 60-month average of 1.99 to 1.76, based on Seeking Alpha data, potentially enhancing the stock’s value. Second, international expansion might increase country risk, conversely weighing on valuation.

Compared with Nike, On already appears attractively valued on a forward basis. 2027 FWD P/E for On is 23x, while Nike’s is 28x. 2028 FWD P/E is 17x, while Nike’s is 23x.

Takeaway

On is firing so strongly on all cylinders that even significant FX headwinds due to a strong Swiss Franc cannot materially weigh on the operational growth dynamics. While still growing strongly in the Americas and with Shoes, EMEA, APAC and Apparel/Accessories expansion are the explosive growth drivers. With that, On rapidly captures relative market share to Nike, but competitive pressure is likely to increase with scale. On the other hand, operating profitability is even expected to improve with scale. Valuation appears fair against the backdrop of On’s growth dynamics. As a result, I remain positive. The Hold rating merely reflects the competitive and fast-paced nature of the business, probably requiring more frequent checking than other Buy-and-Hold investments.

data.title: Silver On Steroids With The Leveraged AGQ ETF
data.description:
In a December 8th Seeking Alpha article on the Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR), I concluded with the following:

I rate SLVR a Buy, as the diversified mining and physical silver ETF provides exposure to the exciting silver bull market. However, with silver at record levels and its penchant for volatility, careful attention to risk-reward dynamics is necessary. In the current environment, buying SLVR requires a plan that accounts for potential downside price risks and profit targets that are a function of the acceptable risk levels.

Market participants seeking silver leverage have other choices. The ProShares Ultra Silver ETF (AGQ) invests in silver futures contracts and seeks to deliver twice the daily performance of the Bloomberg Silver Subindex. While SLVR leverages physical silver and silver mining shares, AQG leverages silver futures.

The parabolic move in silver continues

It did not take long for silver (XAGUSD:CUR) to eclipse the $60 per ounce level after my December 8 Seeking Alpha article. On December 9, silver blew through $60 like a hot knife through butter, and it has kept on rising.

The daily one-year continuous contract COMEX silver futures chart highlights the 121.3% 2025 gain from $29.242 at the end of 2024 to the most recent December 11, 2025, high of $64.72 per ounce. On January 12, 2026, March COMEX silver futures reached a high of $86.34 per ounce.

The technical breakout was a significant event

While silver’s 2025 ascent has been nothing short of spectacular, the technical breakout on the long-term chart has been a significant event.

The quarterly continuous contract chart from 1967 shows that silver prices rose above the 1980 record high of $50.36 in October and continued to rise to higher highs in November, December, and January as the rally turned parabolic.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR), silver’s precious sibling, rose above its 1980 $875 per ounce peak seventeen years ago in 2008. It took silver a long time to catch up to gold and surpass the 1980 peak. However, gold prices have continued to rise, and at over $4,600 per ounce on the active month February 2026 COMEX gold futures contract, the yellow precious metal is now over 5.27 times higher than the 1980 peak. Silver could still have plenty of upside price potential if gold’s ascent is a model.

The chart highlights gold’s price divided by silver’s price, or the silver-gold ratio. In 1980, when gold and silver reached the pre-2008 highs, the ratio fell to 14.24 ounces of silver for each ounce of gold value. At over $4,600 per ounce, a return to that level would put silver prices over $320 per ounce. Meanwhile, the ratio has declined from 1:124.648 in early 2020 to just under 1:54 on January 12, 2026. Technical support for the ratio is now at the Q2 2011 low of 1:31.662.

Bullish trends can defy technical and fundamental analysis

Commodities are volatile assets that can defy reasonable, rational, and logical technical and fundamental analysis when bullish trends develop. Therefore, picking tops in bull markets is more than dangerous; it can be financially deadly. Selling silver short in the current environment amounts to standing in front of a super-powered freight train.

Gold and silver are hardly normal or standard commodities, as they have unique roles as industrial metals and financial assets. For thousands of years, gold and silver have been means of exchange and stores of value. Long before there were dollars, euros, pounds, yen, or any other foreign exchange instruments, gold and silver were hard currency, or money. The price action in the precious metals signals the deterioration of fiat currency values, which derive their values from the full faith and credit in the governments that issue the legal tender. Therefore, the bullish trends and parabolic moves in gold and silver are an unprecedented event that could render fundamental and technical analysis useless. However, even the most aggressive bull markets rarely move in straight lines.

Gravity- The factors that could cause sudden corrections

Commodity cyclicality is a core concept that causes raw material prices to rise to levels where production increases, inventories begin to increase, consumers limit or suspend buying, and prices reach tops and reverse lower. Time will tell if commodity cyclicality eventually impacts gold and silver prices, given their unique roles in the financial system.

When it comes to silver, the following factors could cause an eventual gravitational pull that creates a price top and reversal:

  • While silver is in a fundamental deficit with demand outstripping supply, and investor and speculative buying is exacerbating the demand, there are significant above-ground stocks held by individuals that could increasingly cause dishoarding of silver jewelry, ornamentals, and other products to refiners, increasing the supplies at the highest nominal prices in history.
  • Governments and regulators could step in to limit purchases of gold and silver as concerns about the deterioration of fiat currency values rise.
  • Commodity cyclicality could eventually creep into the precious metals markets as prices rise to unprecedented levels, limiting demand.

The bottom line is that the higher prices rise, the greater the odds of significant downside price corrections.

Riding the silver bull with the leveraged AGQ ETF

While the odds of a silver price correction have increased, the trend remains bullish, and higher highs are likely before silver runs out of upside steam. Gold could be the best example for silver as the price ran through the 1980 high seventeen years ago, continues to reach new record highs, and is over five times the 1980 peak in late 2025, with some analysts calling for $5,000 or higher in 2026.

Gold closed 2024 at $2,641 per ounce after reaching a high of $2,801.80 in Q4 2024. In 2025, gold appreciated 66.5% at the 2025 high of $4,398 per ounce. Gold has already surpassed last year’s high in early 2026, posting its tenth consecutive quarterly new all-time high. With gold above $4,600, a similar move in 2026 would put the yellow metal above $7,000 per ounce. The bottom line is that we have to respect the trends in gold and silver in early 2026, which remain bullish and parabolic.

The fund summary for the ProShares Ultra Silver ETF states:

At $226.75 per share, AGQ had $2.69 billion in assets under management. AGQ trades an average of over 6.5 million shares daily and charges a 0.95% management fee.

The six-month chart of COMEX March silver futures highlights the 131.54% rally from the July 31, 2025, low of $37.29 to the January 12, 2026, high of $86.34 per ounce.

Over the same period, the leveraged AGQ ETF rose 378.11% from $47.79 to $228.49 per share, delivering nearly triple the percentage gain in March COMEX silver futures.

The chart shows that the silver rally has caused the highest possible A+ Seeking Alpha ETF grade for AGQ in the momentum and liquidity categories. Meanwhile, AGQ’s high management fee earns a D- in the expense category, and the risk of the highly volatile silver market earns a failing F grade in the risk category.

AGQ is a tool suitable only for short-term risk positions on the long side of the silver futures market. When approaching leveraged bullish silver market exposure with AGQ, time and price stops are appropriate, as the gearing can lead to oversized losses. Moreover, stable levels in silver will likely cause AGQ to decline, as the time decay erodes its value. The swaps and options that create leverage dramatically increase risks. The SEC website highlights the significant risks associated with leveraged ETFs, such as AGQ. Moreover, AGQ can experience periodic reverse splits, as in January 2014, making it a very short-term trading tool.

Silver’s bullish freight train continues, and the trend is always a trader’s or investor’s best friend. If silver futures are heading for $100 per ounce or higher, AGQ will continue to turbocharge the upside on a percentage basis.

data.title: USFR: Cash Still Makes Sense
data.description:
The WisdomTree Floating Rate Treasury Fund ETF (USFR) was launched on February 4, 2014, by WisdomTree, Inc. and co-managed by WisdomTree Asset Management, Inc. and Mellon Investments Corporation. USFR provides exposure to short-term U.S. Treasury securities whose interest rate resets on a weekly basis based on the latest 13-week T-bill auction. Its assets under management have grown to $16.4 billion, and it charges an expense ratio of 0.15%.

Though the labor market has significantly deteriorated over the last year and this has prompted the Fed to increase the pace of rate cuts in order to avoid a recession, long duration still isn't a good segment to transition to. Cash-like instruments such as this one that realize very low volatility due to the floating-rate feature are still a decent way to park cash.

Portfolio/Performance

The portfolio's duration is 0.02 years and the SEC 30-day yield is currently 3.61%, practically the effective policy rate at this moment. Because of this, price volatility is negligible and USFR can be seen as a pure monetary policy play, making it most sensible to hold when the Fed is hawkish. The keyword here is "most" because in the last 12 months, even as the central bank has been cutting and USFR has been gradually losing its income power, the fund underperformed the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) by a very small margin. More importantly, TLT struggled to consistently outperform throughout last year and due to its long duration, volatility has been very high compared to that realized by USFR.

Outlook

First, I'll need to note that there's no way to talk about the outlook unless the discussion is about the relative advantage short duration has right now.

In past articles, I have emphasized my bias towards short-duration vehicles like USFR instead of long-duration ones like TLT. This hasn't changed, even though the conditions point to a faster pace of easing ahead. Let's see why USFR is an attractive way to park cash instead of increasing duration risk right now.

With the labor market rapidly deteriorating, the Federal Reserve had to pick up the pace of rate cuts late last year. This caused TLT to suddenly start outperforming USFR to a significant degree, as you can see above. By the end of the year, however, the return differential narrowed again, resulting in an interesting twist for Treasury performance.

The first thing to understand is that the regime has changed. Late last year, the term premium widened further because the market refused to get too excited about a dovish Fed.

A contributor to that has been the outlook on fiscal policy, which indicates persisting large deficits as the baseline scenario. This implies more debt issuance and supply, which the market takes into account. This might explain why the long-duration rates remained stubbornly high. With a central bank neutral about absorbing that potential supply, the market is justifiably not too confident these days.

The other issue that offsets the effect of more rate cuts is inflation. This reminds me why the bond market is viewed as more sophisticated than the equity market by some. Although the latest CPI report has shown that inflation has moderated in the past few months, bringing the YoY change to 2.8% and closer to the 2% target set by the Fed, the tariff narrative and a recency bias are bearish factors for long duration. A gradual deflation trend will, of course, change things, but until this forms, long-duration investors will keep demanding higher yields.

Because there's a lot of talk about the labor market, I'll also need to clarify that unless the recession risk increases rapidly and markets panic, the bearish case against long duration doesn't weaken. An orderly decrease in short-term rates to attempt a soft landing is still the most likely path, and that cannot possibly be bullish for long duration.

The argument against USFR and other short-duration ETFs is that they are going to keep losing their income power. Based on the monetary policy path, I do not disagree with this. But if the question is what offers the best risk-adjusted potential right now, the answer remains the same: short duration.

Conclusion

In some articles in the recent past, I have noted that those who can tolerate more volatility may find vehicles that offer exposure to 5-10-year Treasuries more attractive than very short-duration like USFR. That is still the case because they offer a nice balance between long and short duration in an environment that the risk in long duration has slightly decreased due to more rapid rate cuts. But for most investors and purposes, funds like USFR provide a simple and less risky way to have fixed-income exposure. I am rating it a buy.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So, if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

data.title: MoneyShow's Best Investment Ideas For 2026: Part 2
data.description:
MoneyShow’s top contributing analysts, strategists, and newsletter editors share their investment recommendations for the year ahead. See Part 1 here.

Axsome Therapeutics Inc.

By Mike Cintolo, Chief Investment Strategist at Cabot Wealth Network

Axsome Therapeutics Inc. (AXSM) is a name we started to follow just as the market was going over the falls in early 2025. As opposed to the market, the stock stayed down for months afterward. But after a lot of base-building, buyers have taken control and the growth and the story are as good as ever.

As opposed to many biotechs, Axsome isn’t just a one-drug outfit. It has three products on the market now and more to come.

Auvelity (for major depressive disorder) is the big draw these days, with sales up 69% in Q3. Management thinks peak sales could be $2 billion or so, about four times what it brings right now on a run-rate basis. Then, there’s Sunosi for sleep apnea (sales up 35% last quarter) and Symbravo (acute migraines) that only recently hit the market. Each likely has peak sales potential near $400 million down the road.

Beyond that, there are two drugs that are likely to get FDA approval in 2026. One (AXS-12, targeting narcolepsy) looks like a $500 million opportunity. But the bigger one (AXS-05, for Alzheimer’s agitation) should have potential into the $1.5 billion range or more (approval seen likely in the first half of 2026). Axsome even has another drug in Phase III trials (for ADHD and other indications) that it thinks can be another $1 billion producer.

As for the here and now, sales growth is rapid (up 63% and 72% the past two quarters), with analysts looking for 50%-plus gains all of next year. The bottom line is still in the red. But that should change sometime in 2026 as business continues to ramp. Plus, sales should continue to grow rapidly for years to come even beyond this year.

As mentioned above, the stock etched a big launching pad, consolidating for about nine months in total. But it’s been acting much better since late September, with shares moving past their prior highs in late 2025. Near-term volatility is possible, but we think the path of least resistance is up - and approvals of the AXS drugs could provide an added tailwind.

Recommended Action: Buy AXSM.

Becton, Dickinson & Co.

By Kelley Wright, Editor of IQ Trends

Becton, Dickinson & Co. (BDX) is a global medical technology company that develops, manufactures, and sells a wide range of medical supplies, devices, laboratory equipment, and diagnostic products. It represents an attractive total return opportunity for 2026.

BDX operates through five segments: Medical Essentials, Connected Care, BioPharma Systems, Interventional, and Life Sciences. BDX’s product offerings include peripheral intravenous catheters, medication delivery and management systems, pre-fillable drug delivery systems, and advanced patient monitoring systems.

The company provides solutions for healthcare institutions, life science researchers, clinical laboratories, and the pharmaceutical industry on a global scale. The company’s operations extend across multiple geographies, including manufacturing facilities in the US, China, and Germany, among others.

The historically repetitive high dividend (Undervalue) yield is 1.6%. Based on the current dividend of $4.20, that equates to a $255 share price. Recently trading around $192 per share, the current yield was about 2.1%. That represents a 33% discount from its historically repetitive Undervalue area.

The historically repetitive low dividend (Overvalue) yield is 1.1%. Again, based on the current dividend of $4.20, that equates to $355 per share, which represents a theoretical 90% upside.

The ROIC (return on invested capital) is 10%. The FCFY (free cash flow yield) is 4%, and the P/EBV (price dividend by economic book value) is 0.9. Economic Earnings Per Share = $6.45 vs $5.81 reported (GAAP). The Economic Book Value = $217.66 per share.

My thought is the negatives expressed by the analytical community have been fully baked into the current stock price, but the internal cash flow measures paint a more positive picture.

Recommended Action: Buy BDX.

Berkshire Hathaway Inc.

By Albert Lu, President of Luma Financial

Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) is a large and diversified US business conglomerate, with operations that include insurance, transportation, and energy, among others. It is a core holding in our client portfolios. Here’s why.

The company, founded in 1839 as a textile manufacturer, has operated in its present form since 1965, with Warren Buffett, the chairman and CEO, at its helm. From inception to 2024, the company has achieved an average annual compounded gain of 19.9%, compared with 10.4% for the S&P 500 Index(SPX).

Official US statistics put annual inflation at around 3% from September 2024 to September 2025, but some experience suggests the actual rate is much higher. For example, from 2014 to 2024, the cost of a McDouble hamburger at McDonald’s increased from $1.19 to $3.19 - a rate of increase corresponding to roughly 10% per year. If this continues much longer, they will have to rename it the McTriple.

With Treasuries yielding just above 4%, even conservative investors have no choice but to seek alternatives, such as stocks, to protect their purchasing power. The challenge then becomes shielding your nest egg from the ups and downs of the market. In this case, a large, diversified business with stable profits and a solid balance sheet is just what the doctor ordered.

Meanwhile, as investor anxiety rises over the potential of a popping Artificial Intelligence (AI) bubble, Berkshire Hathaway presents an attractive alternative to technology stocks and market-weighted indexes. While the company does maintain some exposure to AI through its public investments, its exposure to AI-related semiconductor companies or AI pure-plays is minimal to non-existent.

In its Q3 2025 report, Berkshire Hathaway reported cash and cash equivalents in the combined businesses of over $380 billion. While the large cash hoard has been the target of criticism, we view it as a strategic asset. Turbulent times sometimes present rare profit opportunities. If the market is indeed heading for a big correction, this company has the experience and balance sheet to capitalize.

Finally, President Trump recently announced that the US had secured up to $21 trillion of investment capital, a number that corresponds to the largest capital-spending boom in post-war US history. While the actual number is a topic of debate (Bloomberg puts the figure closer to $7 trillion), one thing is certain - the country needs capital to create jobs and restore its infrastructure and manufacturing base.

Berkshire Hathaway has a track record of working with the government in tough times. Whether it’s developing hydrogen energy or navigating a financial crisis, Uncle Sam knows he can count on Berkshire Hathaway.

Blackstone Inc.

By Stephen Biggar, Analyst at Argus Research

We believe that Blackstone Inc. (BX) has strong, long-term underlying earnings potential, particularly from growth in fee-based AUM, which should benefit from more fund vehicles becoming fee-eligible.

Blackstone had a very healthy $225 billion of fundraising in the 12 months ended September 30, 2025, which we believe speaks to the considerable capabilities and brand name of the franchise. Meanwhile, the company had $188 billion in dry powder at the end of Q3 2025, allowing it to take advantage of market dislocations.

We believe BX has numerous opportunities to put funds to work, including in real estate, private equity, infrastructure, tactical opportunities, and credit assets. We note that Blackstone also had $501 billion of perpetual capital at the end of Q3 2025, up 15% from the prior year, mostly in private equity and credit and insurance. This enables the company to make long-term decisions and to avoid selling at inappropriate times.

In addition, management recently highlighted a robust fund disposition pipeline amid an improving transaction backdrop. The firm believes realization potential is significant in 2026, helped by a lower interest rate environment and record stock prices.

The company also pays a better-than-3% dividend yield. Plus, we expect a healthy increase in the 2026 dividend payment, along with our forecast for an 18% rise in distributable earnings for the year.

Recommended Action: Buy BX.

Brown & Brown Inc.

By Ben Reynolds, Editor of Sure Dividend

Brown & Brown Inc. (BRO) is a leading insurance brokerage firm that provides risk management solutions to both individuals and businesses, with a focus on property and casualty insurance. The company employs about 17,400 people, is expected to produce about $5.9 billion in revenue this year, and trades with a market capitalization of $27 billion.

Brown & Brown has increased its dividend payments for 32 consecutive years, including a 10% dividend increase announced on October 23, 2025. Brown & Brown is a member of the exclusive Dividend Aristocrats list, thanks to its long dividend growth streak.

On October 27, 2025, Brown & Brown also announced third-quarter earnings for the period ending September 30, 2025. Adjusted earnings per share grew 15.4% to $1.05. The company continued its long record of strong execution as it integrated more than 5,000 new teammates, expanding its capabilities across markets globally.

Despite Brown & Brown’s long dividend history, it has a payout ratio of just 16%. As a result, the stock recently had a dividend yield below 1%, making it more compelling for growth-focused investors than yield-focused investors.

But the low payout ratio also means the insurer has ample funds to reinvest for growth. Brown & Brown’s growth strategy is both simple and sustainable. The company actively acquires smaller insurance brokerage firms and integrates them into its larger operating base.

Brown & Brown has an impressive growth record, as it has grown its EPS by 18% per year on average over the last nine years. Book value per share has grown at a double-digit rate over this period as well. We believe that the company’sstrategy has plenty of room left to run, and expect 11% average annual growth of EPS until 2030.

The company’s stock was recently trading at less than 19 times its projected 2025 earnings. We believe Brown & Brown deserves to trade at a premium multiple given its safety and growth prospects. Our fair value P/E ratio is 24.

As a result, we believe the company to be meaningfully undervalued. The combination of strong growth, safety, and an undervalued stock price makes Brown & Brown a compelling purchase to kick off 2026.

Disclosure: Ben Reynolds is long BRO.

Cal-Maine Foods Inc.

By Nilus Mattive, Editor of Safe Money Report

While most of the world has been fixated on a handful of overpriced, overhyped tech stocks, Safe Money Report readers have been doing very well with economically insensitive stocks that pay big dividends. When I performed a screen using the Weiss Ratings system looking for highly rated consumer staples companies, Cal-Maine Foods Inc. (CALM) jumped out at me right away.

I don’t see any reason for things to change going forward, especially if the companies I target can keep hiking their prices at - or above - general inflation rates. With CALM, it’s categorized as a packaged foods company, but eggs are its specialty.

I probably don’t need to tell you that eggs became the universal symbol of rising prices when inflation was really soaring... or that people still kept on buying them despite all the complaining. That’s precisely why I’ve been recommending the company as a solid holding going forward.

Cal-Maine is actually the largest publicly traded egg producer in the US, with 48 million egg-laying hens and annual egg sales of roughly 1.3 billion. The company’s revenue soared along with egg prices. And even though one of the big driving forces - an outbreak of the H5N1 avian flu - has started to fade, prices are still in line with levels from two years ago.

Cal-Maine also has an extra advantage: Its leading position in free-range and organic egg categories. That gives it a great segment of the market where well-heeled customers are usually willing to pay even higher-than-average prices.

All that said, Cal-Maine’s future earnings and profits are relatively hard to predict. A lot will depend on where egg prices go. And investors were actually pushing the stock even lower into the end of 2025. But at less than four times the company’s earnings over the last twelve months, profits could get cut in half and the shares would still be trading at a very reasonable single-digit P/E!

Meanwhile, the company adjusts its dividends every quarter so that they equal one-third of quarterly income. I expect they will at least match - and probably exceed - what US Treasurys will produce over the next year or two. And unlike a bond’s yield, CALM’s yield can keep on climbing the longer you hold.

Given its reasonable valuation, solid business, and ongoing dividends, I think this is a great conservative play for 2026 and beyond. And the Weiss Ratings system agrees, giving CALM a solid B- “Buy” rating recently.

Recommended Action: Buy CALM.

Cassiar Gold Corp.

By Brien Lundin, Executive Editor of Gold Newsletter

Cassiar Gold Corp. (CGLCF) is the kind of still-underappreciated value that’s rare in this stage of a gold bull market. The company controls a district-scale, 590-square kilometer land package in northern British Columbia.

It’s anchored by a 2.34 million-ounce, near-surface, bulk-tonnage resource at the Taurus deposit, with 91% of the ounces sitting within 150 meters of surface. Most of that land position remains sparsely drilled, yet the stock still trades at a modest market valuation that works out to a very low in-situ value of only around $14/ounce of gold resource.

In a market where many similar names have already doubled or tripled, Cassiar has seen only a modest move off the lows. In our view, that leaves it as one of the better long-term value propositions in the junior gold space.

The company’s updated NI 43-101 technical report on the Cassiar Gold Property pegs Taurus at 8.8 million tonnes grading 1.43 g/t gold for 410,000 ounces in the indicated category, plus 63.2 million tonnes at 0.95 g/t for 1.93 million ounces inferred, using a 0.4 g/t cut-off and a $2,400/oz. gold price. Obviously, there’s lots of resource upside available at current gold prices.

The resource is pit-constrained, open for expansion in multiple directions, and already has strong strip and geometry working in its favor. That alone underpins the current valuation. But the real upside lies in the combination of emerging high-grade domains at Taurus, the Newcoast target just to the south, and a long trend of largely untested, Taurus-style geology across the broader Cassiar North and Cassiar South project areas.

Layer on the rest of the land package and you start to see the leverage. Cassiar South hosts multiple high-grade, past-producing orogenic veins that delivered more than 315,000 ounces at 10 g/t-20 g/t historically. The company also controls most of the high-grade Sheep Creek camp in southern British Columbia, which produced over 700,000 ounces at nearly 15 g/t but has seen little modern exploration. None of that is in the current resource, and relatively little modern drilling has gone into these targets compared with the scale of the ground.

The balance sheet is also funded, management is visible in the market, and the stock is still trading at a level that doesn’t come close to fully valuing the current ounces in the ground. Cassiar remains a buy for patient investors looking for underpriced leverage to both the gold price and resource growth, with the potential for a significant re-rating as the market catches up to the scale of the opportunity.

Recommended Action: Buy CGLCF.

Originally posted on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: This Silver Indicator Suggests An Upside That Few Are Believing
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on assets that track the performance of the main hard assets. This article is part of a weekly series that started on January 6th where I comment on the hard assets scenario and bring valuable insights for investors.

In this article, we will look at relevant insights into nickel, copper, silver, and gold. Additionally, an event will occur soon that could bring volatility to hard assets, which could create opportunities for attentive investors.

Context

Last week again brought strong gains to hard assets. For reference, we saw an all-time high in copper and were very close to the all-time high for gold. However, the week also was one of gains for oil, silver, platinum, nickel, and even uranium. Let's see below a summary of the gains in hard assets.

In my view, the asset that deserves the most attention in these variations is oil. The last week saw the asset's biggest daily gain since October. Clearly prices react to an expectation of an increase in stocks combined with an increase in geopolitical tensions, especially in Iran. I delve into the thesis in more detail in another article.

After looking in the rearview mirror, we can now focus on what really matters: the prospects for hard assets. In this sense, I separated four insights into four different hard assets. Let's talk about nickel, copper, gold, and silver.

Nickel Returns to the Spotlight

About 10 years ago, nickel was considered one of the most strategic hard assets, given its relevance in the production of batteries for electric vehicles. This dynamic caused prices to soar in 2022; however, the story did not unfold as expected.

What was seen after 2022 was a large increase in production (due to expectations), combined with a lower-than-expected uptake of electric vehicles. This caused prices to plummet from $30,000 per ton to less than $15,000 per ton in less than 4 years.

But now, the story may be different. Indonesia is the world's largest nickel producer and recently announced it will reduce supply by up to 34% in an effort to boost metal prices. As governments increasingly seek to increase their revenue, the measure could be a tailwind for nickel prices.

I explain in more detail the impacts of this and the consequences for the largest nickel producer in the world, Vale (NYSE:VALE) in another article. Now, let's talk about copper.

Cheap copper according to expectations?

While copper reached a new ATH at $13k per ton last week, S&P Global released a curious article. The institution projects a deficit of 10 million tons of copper by 2040. The argument is that while there will be explosive demand (28 million tons to 42 million tons) due to electric vehicles, AI and data centers, supply will peak in 2030, and new projects take decades to come to fruition.

I do not yet have a specific thesis on copper, but it is already in the process of being created, however, I want to leave a reminder for investors. You read the previous chapter, right? In it, I say that nickel prices soared in 2022 due to electrification expectations and fell 50% after 4 years as the thesis was not fulfilled. Therefore, you should be careful about FOMO and adjust your portfolio considering risks and returns. Now, let's talk about silver and close with gold.

Is Silver Expensive?

Many investors think that silver has become expensive after its record high in 2025. Last week I brought unique arguments to prove that the precious metal is not expensive, and my research to create this week's article revealed another interesting insight in this regard. See the chart below:

When we look at the Silver-to-Dow Ratio chart, we see that there is a lot of room for silver to appreciate. If the target is the 2011 peak, for example, there is potential for silver to rise 175% and outperform 2025 when it rose 145%.

Gold Approaches ATH

Gold is without a doubt the most relevant precious metal, and here I bring two relevant insights. The first is related to seasonality, and the second to speculative investors.

As we can see below, we have the gold seasonality chart. Historically, the first four months of the year tend to deliver relatively strong performances compared to the middle of the year.

But the insight that caught my attention the most will be explained below. The largest futures positions, mostly used by speculators, are gradually moving towards commodities. As we can see below, we have gold, nickel, gasoline, silver, and copper.

But particularly the second position caught my attention. The second largest position is in BRL, the currency of Brazil, a country characterized by holding many commodities. I remember that I offered my recommended stock portfolio for 2026 using many Brazilian companies precisely because I believed that the strong momentum of hard assets would boost these stocks.

Potential Risks To The Thesis

There is a relevant risk for hard assets at the beginning of the year, especially for silver. Several passive commodity indices will rebalance. As we can see below, silver, aluminum, gold, and nickel can show great volatility due to these rebalancing.

The Bottom Line

We saw that the last week showed relevant increases in oil, silver, gold, and platinum. Additionally, we discussed the nickel and copper theses a little more and how the two may have similarities. The point is for investors not to get carried away by FOMO.

We also analyze how the prospects for other and silver remain relevant, whether through comparative analyses or seasonality. We have also seen how currencies from commodity-related countries are on the rise, such as the Brazilian real.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my buy recommendation on assets that track the performance of the main hard assets. The outlook for these assets remains positive, and the volatility that will occur with the rebalancing of indices could create opportunities.

data.title: It's Not 1999: The AI 'Bubble' Has Lots Of Room To Run
data.description:
Thesis Summary

For the last year, people have been screaming about an AI bubble.

Yes, AI equities have rallied hard, and expectations around AI may have exceeded reality.

But that doesn’t mean we are in a bubble, and even if we are, we could still be very far from popping it.

Looking at actual comparisons of the most recent tech bubble, 1999, and today shows that from a fundamental perspective, there’s still a lot more air to be blown into this bubble.

This Is Not 1999

While everyone wants to compare this AI rally to the 1999 tech bubble, there are just too many differences, even today.

Cash Flow vs Capex

A lot has been said about the divergence between the expansion in Capex and the reduction Free cash flow.

While this is true, and it’s something that could be problematic for select companies, the overall tech market is very far from the levels seen in the 90s.

The ratio of Capex-FCF is below 40%, and this is mainly due to the very strong cash flows from the big players.

As we can see, the operating cash flow of the big tech companies still far exceeds Capital Expenditures.

High-Yield Debt Growth

But James, haven’t companies been issuing bonds to pay for this new AI infrastructure rollout? Indeed, they have, and these include megacaps like Meta (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL).

But again, a simple look at 1999 comparables shows that we are likely very far from any danger zone.

The chart above shows high-yield and investment-grade debt growth during 1996-2001 and between 2022 and 2025.

And as we can see, debt use exploded ahead of the market top in 2000. Very different story from today, when debt has grown 9% in 2025.

Are we accelerating? Clearly, yes, but this doesn’t look like a problem

AI productivity

Lastly, though this is harder to quantify, and we have to be skeptical of any projections made today, the pace of AI adoption and productivity growth seems to be outperforming that of the internet.

For starters, from a fundamental perspective, I see AI as a much more natural evolution from today’s technological level.

The internet revolution was jarring and met with significant resistance. But we have lived in a tech-first world for two decades now, and AI is a natural extension that is being embraced by individuals and companies alike.

Adoption continues to increase amongst companies, with nearly 10% of companies now saying they used AI tools in the last two weeks.

And according to Accenture, productivity gains in the US will reach 35% by 2035. We should take this with a grain of salt, but we also can’t deny there is clear evidence of increased productivity across many sectors.

The Risk Factor

The real risk factor now is not that companies go bankrupt in a 1999-style capitulation. Rather, the real risk factor is that the AI buildout slows down due to bottlenecks and supply constraints.

Companies are trading based on ambitious plans that these data centers will be built out, and if this timeline is pushed back, valuations will reflect this.

I’m talking about limitations in commodities, for example, or in certain components, like memory, which has recently been in the news.

A slowdown of the infrastructure rollout could temper enthusiasm and valuations, but if anything, it could also serve to dampen animal spirits and lead to a more balanced AI rollout.

How To Actually Profit

Now, here’s the key issue for investors.

In 2024, the market was chasing AI infrastructure names as everyone rushed to build out data centers. But competition has intensified, and valuations are now high.

Ultimately, I see companies building out data centers as comparable to those laying down fiber optic wire.

They are putting down key infrastructure that will facilitate the next technological revolution, but that doesn’t mean they will be the primary beneficiaries.

Those who rose to fame during the dot-com and its aftermath were those who could successfully build applications on top of the internet layer.

Similarly, the AI winners will be those leveraging the technology, not laying out the infrastructure.

In some cases, they may be one and the same: Google and Meta.

In other cases, it could be pioneers in the sector, like Palantir

And probably, the largest AI-enabled companies of the next decade are working out of a garage right now.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, I don’t think there is clear evidence of an AI bubble.

However, certain AI-related sectors may have run too far and will underperform in 2026.

But AI is still where the real opportunities for high growth and returns are.
